op.name: linalg.eigh
op.summary: Return the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a complex Hermitian (conjugate symmetric) or a real symmetric matrix.

blocks.text:
Return the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a complex Hermitian
(conjugate symmetric) or a real symmetric matrix.

Returns two objects, a 1-D array containing the eigenvalues of `a`, and
a 2-D square array or matrix (depending on the input type) of the
corresponding eigenvectors (in columns).

desc_blocks.text:
Hermitian or real symmetric matrices whose eigenvalues and
eigenvectors are to be computed.

Specifies whether the calculation is done with the lower triangular
part of `a` ('L', default) or the upper triangular part ('U').
Irrespective of this value only the real parts of the diagonal will
be considered in the computation to preserve the notion of a Hermitian
matrix. It therefore follows that the imaginary part of the diagonal
will always be treated as zero.

items.type: A namedtuple with the following attributes:

desc_blocks.text:
The eigenvalues in ascending order, each repeated according to
its multiplicity.

items.name: eigenvectors
items.type: {(..., M, M) ndarray, (..., M, M) matrix}

desc_blocks.text:
The column ``eigenvectors[:, i]`` is the normalized eigenvector
corresponding to the eigenvalue ``eigenvalues[i]``.  Will return a
matrix object if `a` is a matrix object.

blocks.text:
Broadcasting rules apply, see the `numpy.linalg` documentation for
details.

The eigenvalues/eigenvectors are computed using LAPACK routines ``_syevd``,
``_heevd``.

The eigenvalues of real symmetric or complex Hermitian matrices are always
real. [1]_ The array `eigenvalues` of (column) eigenvectors is unitary and
`a`, `eigenvalues`, and `eigenvectors` satisfy the equations ``dot(a,
eigenvectors[:, i]) = eigenvalues[i] * eigenvectors[:, i]``.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from numpy import linalg as LA
>>> a = np.array([[1, -2j], [2j, 5]])
>>> a
array([[ 1.+0.j, -0.-2.j],
       [ 0.+2.j,  5.+0.j]])
>>> eigenvalues, eigenvectors = LA.eigh(a)
>>> eigenvalues
array([0.17157288, 5.82842712])
>>> eigenvectors
array([[-0.92387953+0.j        , -0.38268343+0.j        ], # may vary
       [ 0.        +0.38268343j,  0.        -0.92387953j]])

>>> (np.dot(a, eigenvectors[:, 0]) -
... eigenvalues[0] * eigenvectors[:, 0])  # verify 1st eigenval/vec pair
array([5.55111512e-17+0.0000000e+00j, 0.00000000e+00+1.2490009e-16j])
>>> (np.dot(a, eigenvectors[:, 1]) -
... eigenvalues[1] * eigenvectors[:, 1])  # verify 2nd eigenval/vec pair
array([0.+0.j, 0.+0.j])

>>> A = np.matrix(a) # what happens if input is a matrix object
>>> A
matrix([[ 1.+0.j, -0.-2.j],
        [ 0.+2.j,  5.+0.j]])
>>> eigenvalues, eigenvectors = LA.eigh(A)
>>> eigenvalues
array([0.17157288, 5.82842712])
>>> eigenvectors
matrix([[-0.92387953+0.j        , -0.38268343+0.j        ], # may vary
        [ 0.        +0.38268343j,  0.        -0.92387953j]])

blocks.text: eigvalsh — eigenvalues of real symmetric or complex Hermitian (conjugate symmetric) arrays.
eig — eigenvalues and right eigenvectors for non-symmetric arrays.
scipy.linalg.eigh — Similar function in SciPy (but also solves the generalized eigenvalue problem).
